title: Technical and Professional Writing
Technical and Professional Writing
Technical and Professional Writing
text:
2 Start with Audience and Purpose
Before writing, identify two things:

Who is the reader?
What should the document accomplish?









Document
Likely Reader
Primary Purpose




Résumé
Employer, recruiter, hiring manager, research supervisor
Show that your background fits a specific role.


Online professional profile
Employers, alumni, collaborators, graduate programs, professional contacts
Present a concise and credible professional identity.


Research proposal
Fellowship reviewers, faculty, research mentors
Persuade the reader that a research question is important, feasible, and worth supporting.


Personal statement
Graduate admissions committee, faculty group, research supervisor
Explain your preparation, goals, and fit for a specific academic opportunity.


Cover letter
Employer or hiring committee
Show why your experience and interests match a specific job posting.



A document that is technically polished but not shaped toward its actual audience will usually be less effective than a slightly simpler document that is highly specific and well targeted.
An example mismatch would be a B.S. Physics student applying to a data analysis, engineering, or technical role who writes a résumé like a graduate-school application, emphasizing abstract intellectual interests and course topics while underemphasizing concrete skills, projects, software and instrumentation, teamwork, and deliverables.

title: Technical and Professional Writing
section: 3 Writing Style: Clear, Specific, and Direct
text:
3 Writing Style: Clear, Specific, and Direct
Technical and professional writing should favor clarity over vagueness, needless complexity, and ornament for its own sake. Use specialized vocabulary when your audience requires it, but do not use complexity to imitate expertise.

3.1 Prefer Specific Claims







Less Effective
More Effective




I gained valuable research experience.
I analyzed detector calibration data in Python and developed plots used in weekly group meetings.


This research is important.
This work addresses how improved vacuum diagnostics could improve pump longevity and increase vacuum stability.


I am passionate about physics.
My interest in condensed-matter developed through my coursework in quantum mechanics, auditing a graduate-level solid-state class, and a summer project with Professor X modeling lattice excitations.





3.2 Use Strong Verbs
Especially in résumés and cover letters, strong verbs help the reader understand what you did. Examples include analyzed, designed, developed, implemented, measured, modeled, presented, programmed, tested, and wrote.


3.3 Avoid Empty Intensifiers
Words such as very, extremely, incredibly, and highly often add less value than a concrete detail.






TipBetter habit



Replace vague emphasis with evidence, examples, or measurable detail whenever possible.

title: Technical and Professional Writing
text:
4 Concision and Structure
Concise writing is about sentence structure rather than whether a document is particularly lengthy or short.

4.1 Paragraph Structure
Each paragraph should usually have:

A clear main point;
Specific support or evidence;
A connection back to the purpose of the document.

For example, in a personal statement, a paragraph about prior research experience should not merely list tasks. It should explain how those tasks prepared you for the graduate program, professor, laboratory, or research direction you are targeting.

title: Technical and Professional Writing
section: 5 Formatting: Make the Document Easy to Read
text:
5 Formatting: Make the Document Easy to Read
Formatting helps a reader navigate your document, locate key information, and form an impression of your professionalism. This last effect is partly psychological and partly conventional: a reader may ignore your message or stop reading if the document is difficult or annoying to read (the same is true of professors grading your work!). Formatting may be precisely prescribed, as in a grant proposal, or guided more loosely by disciplinary norms and aesthetic judgment, as in an academic publication.

5.1 General Formatting Principles

Use consistent spacing, indentation, capitalization, and punctuation.
Use headings to guide the reader where appropriate.
Maintain enough white space that the page does not feel crowded.
Choose readable fonts and avoid excessive visual complexity.
Align dates, titles, and section labels consistently.

title: Technical and Professional Writing
text:
6 Citations and References
Citations are especially important in research writing. They show how your work fits into existing knowledge, help establish credibility, and allow readers to confirm information used to support your claims. Citations should also prioritize primary sources, peer-reviewed literature, and authoritative texts over blogs, social media posts, and anonymous webpages.

6.1 When Should You Cite?
In a research document, whether a proposal or publication, cite sources when you:

Describe prior research or accepted findings;
Identify an open problem, technical limitation, or gap in the literature;
Use a specialized method, model, dataset, or established experimental approach;
Make a factual claim that is not common knowledge;
Position your proposed research relative to existing work.







WarningDo not pad references



Unless you are writing a review article, a compact and well-chosen bibliography is usually more effective than a long list of sources that are never meaningfully integrated into the document’s message.




6.2 Responsible Source Use
Technical writing requires intentional use of references. When using outside sources:

Read sources closely enough to understand what they actually claim.
Do not cite a paper merely because it seems related from the title or abstract.
Do not copy wording without quotation and attribution.
Do not distort a source to make it support a claim it does not support.
Check citations, bibliographic information, and URLs carefully before submission.

title: Technical and Professional Writing
text:
7 Revision Is Part of Writing
Strong documents are rarely produced in a single draft. Revision should be expected.

7.1 A Useful Revision Process

Draft the main content and get the main ideas onto the page.
Revise the structure to check that the document flows logically.
Revise to replace vague claims with details and evidence.
Revise to ensure the document satisfies the purpose expected by the reader.
Edit the style and formatting to improve clarity and remove awkward sentences.
Proofread carefully.







TipRecommendation



Read important application documents aloud during revision. Awkward phrasing, overly long sentences, and unclear transitions often become easier to notice.

title: Peer Feedback
text:
The typical experience of peer feedback goes something like this: you trade papers with a classmate, quickly skim each other’s work, write “good introduction” and circle a few typos, and call it done. Nothing changes. Nobody learned anything.
That version of peer review fails because the feedback is vague, generic, and not connected to what the document actually needs to do (Roberts 2016). The goal here is feedback that the author can act on, from a reader who has thought seriously about the document.






::: {style=text-align: center; color: #555;“} xkcd #1098 “Qualifications” · CC BY-NC 2.5


::::






Important



Peer feedback is a professional skill. In research, your labmates, collaborators, co-authors, and dissertation committee will all read and critique your work before it reaches a journal or grant panel.

title: Peer Feedback
text:
The typical experience of peer feedback goes something like this: you trade papers with a classmate, quickly skim each other’s work, write “good introduction” and circle a few typos, and call it done. Nothing changes. Nobody learned anything.
That version of peer review fails because the feedback is vague, generic, and not connected to what the document actually needs to do (Roberts 2016). The goal here is feedback that the author can act on, from a reader who has thought seriously about the document.






::: {style=text-align: center; color: #555;“} xkcd #1098 “Qualifications” · CC BY-NC 2.5


::::






Important



Peer feedback is a professional skill. In research, your labmates, collaborators, co-authors, and dissertation committee will all read and critique your work before it reaches a journal or grant panel.

title: Peer Feedback
text:
2 Benefits of Peer Review
Before you begin, it helps to recognize what peer review is actually good for (Kuyyogsuy 2019):

You see how other students interpreted the same assignment and solved the same problems.
You receive feedback from someone with no authority to grade you. The stakes are lower and the conversation can be more honest.
You have a chance to ask questions and get them answered before final submission.
You practice the workforce skill of giving and receiving effective criticism, which every professional environment requires.

title: Peer Feedback
text:
3 Prepare Before You Read
Most of what separates useful feedback from useless feedback is preparation. Taking time to understand how to perform peer review will make your feedback more valuable and give you confidence in giving it (Brammer and Rees 2007).

3.1 Understand Your Position
As a peer. You are at the same level as the author. You have no authority to demand changes. You are there to offer suggestions and recommendations, not edicts. The author decides what to do with your feedback.
As a reader. You may feel unqualified to evaluate another person’s writing, but you already evaluate movies, restaurants, and sports teams without being an expert in any of them. You do this because you know when something works for you as an audience member. You know when you are confused, bored, persuaded, or engaged. That is exactly the qualification you need here.


3.2 Understand the Document
Every document is meant to communicate something for a specific purpose within a set of expected boundaries:

Requirements. What are the absolute requirements? Did the document meet them? Consult the specific requirements of the assignment.
Purpose. What is the document trying to accomplish? Getting a job? Demonstrating a valid research program?
Audience. Who is this document written for? A recruiter? A graduate admissions committee?
Subject. Does the document stay on topic? Are there sections, arguments, or details that do not serve the document’s stated purpose?







Note



A résumé here could go to an actual employer. A personal statement could go to an actual graduate program. Evaluate the document against the real-world audience it is intended to reach.




3.3 Understand the Author
The better you understand who the author is, the more useful your feedback will be.

Goals. What is the author trying to achieve with this document? A résumé aimed at research roles should be evaluated differently than one aimed at industry.
Voice. Does the document sound like the person who wrote it? If the tone shifts abruptly or something feels borrowed, that is worth noting.
What they need. Some authors need high-level structural direction; others need confirmation they are on the right track. Calibrate your feedback to what will actually help.

title: Peer Feedback
text:
4 Providing Feedback
All feedback ultimately boils down to the following logical construction: “My reaction is X, because of Y.” This structure is how all your major feedback, both positive and negative, should be delivered.






Tip



The word “because” (or equivalent language) is an excellent way to link your reaction to a document and the reason for your reaction.



4.1 Make Positive Comments
It matters that the author hears what is working. Praise that is specific helps the author know which choices to keep and repeat.







Weak
Strong




“Good introduction.”
“The opening is good because it immediately names the specific lab technique you want to improve and why it is important.”


“I liked the research section.”
“The research motivation connects your goal directly to an open question in the field, not just a general area of interest.”



You often will know whether or not you like a piece of writing before you’ve consciously identified why. Don’t fret if it takes you a little time to pinpoint the reason why you like something. This sort of self-reflection is a powerful skill.


4.2 Give Constructive Criticism
While simple critique still follows the “My reaction is X, because of Y” formula, constructive criticism takes it one step further by offering a potential solution: “My reaction is X, because of Y, therefore I suggest Z.”






Note



While we should always strive towards constructive criticism, it is inherently more difficult. Sometimes you’ll only have the time and mental bandwidth to point out issues without necessarily knowing how to fix things, especially if the issues are extensive.


Constructive criticism gives the author a direction for how to make their work better. Focus your criticism in three layers:

Requirements. Did the document meet the stated requirements? If something is missing, point it out directly. Those are points the author will lose (or an immediate job rejection) if you do not flag them.
Meaning. Is it clear what the document is saying? Are claims supported with evidence? Are there places where you wanted more, or where you still had unanswered questions? These are the comments that matter most for revision.
Speed bumps. A speed bump is anything that stops your reading: a sentence that confused you, a transition that did not land, a word choice that felt off, a section that seemed to lose focus. Pay attention to what your brain does as you read. It will usually signal when something needs revision.







Tip



You do not have to be right. Your job is to report what you experienced as a reader. The author decides whether to act on your observation.

title: Peer Feedback
section: 5 How to Structure Your Written Response
text:
5 How to Structure Your Written Response
A clear structure makes your feedback easier to use.

Opening summary. One or two sentences on your overall impression. What is working? What is the most important thing to address?
Major comments. Two to four substantive observations about content, structure, audience fit, or meaning. Identify a specific location in the document for each comment, explain the issue, and suggest a direction for improvement.
Minor comments. Smaller issues: phrasing, formatting, word choice, typos. Worth noting, but should not dominate your response unless such issues are severe.
Closing. What is the document’s strongest aspect? What single revision would most improve it?

Spend the most time on the major comments. A review that is mostly a list of typos is not helpful as proofreading is the author’s responsibility. What they need is your perspective on whether the document is doing its job.

title: Peer Feedback
text:
6 Common Mistakes in Peer Review








Mistake
Why It Fails
Better Approach




“This looks great!”
Offers nothing the author can act on
Identify something specific that works and something that could be stronger


“This is confusing.”
Does not say where or why
Quote the specific passage and explain what confused you


“I would have done it differently…”
Imposes your approach
Ask whether the author’s choice serves their purpose, then offer an alternative as a suggestion


Fixing only surface errors
Misses content and structure, which matter more
Address meaning and organization first, then surface polish


Softening every criticism
Author cannot tell what actually needs work
State problems clearly while keeping the tone professional

title: Peer Feedback
text:
7 Receiving Feedback
You will also receive peer feedback on your own documents. How you receive it matters.

Listen without immediately defending. You do not need to agree with every comment, but understand what the reviewer is responding to before you decide whether to act on it.
Ask clarifying questions if you do not understand what a comment is pointing to.
Separate the diagnosis from the solution. A reviewer may correctly identify a problem but suggest a fix that does not fit your document. Take the diagnosis seriously even if you choose a different approach.
You are not obligated to make every suggested change, but you should be able to explain to yourself why you are not making it.







NoteFeedback is information, not instruction



A reviewer’s job is to tell you how the document is landing on a reader. What you do with that information is your decision.

title: Peer Feedback
text:
8 A Note on Tone
Professional feedback should be direct but not unkind. You can be honest without being dismissive, and specific without being harsh. The standard to aim for is the kind of feedback a thoughtful colleague or mentor would give.
Sharing your writing may make you feel vulnerable, which is completely normal. Feedback that is honest and encouraging is one of the most useful things you can offer a peer.

title: Peer Feedback
text:
9 References


Brammer, Charlotte, and Mary Rees. 2007. “Peer Review from the Students’ Perspective: Invaluable or Invalid?” Composition Studies 35 (2): 71–85. https://www.jstor.org/stable/43501704.


Kuyyogsuy, Sirikarn. 2019. “Promoting Peer Feedback in Developing Students’ English Writing Ability in L2 Writing Class.” International Education Studies 12 (9): 76–88. https://doi.org/10.5539/ies.v12n9p76.


Roberts, Jason. 2016. “Making a Peer Review More Than a Waste of Time.” In Open English @ SLCC. SLCC English Department. https://pressbooks.pub/openenglishatslcc/chapter/making-a-peer-review-more-than-a-waste-of-time/.









NoteLicense



The peer review framework presented in this page is adapted from Jason Roberts, “Making a Peer Review More Than a Waste of Time,” in Open English @ SLCC (Roberts 2016). Open English @ SLCC Copyright © 2016 by SLCC English Department. Licensed under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License.

title: Syllabus
text:
Fall 2026 | CRN #94323 | 1 credit hour
Fridays, 4:00–4:50 PM • Howey Physics L3 • Weekly in-person attendance required

title: Syllabus
text:
1 Course Description
In this course, we will discuss practical skills for post-degree success. Topics include selecting graduate schools, understanding career options with a B.S. or Ph.D., applying to graduate school, applying to industry jobs, developing a professional network, and preparing a research proposal suitable for the NSF Graduate Research Fellowship Program.
NSF Graduate Research Fellowship Program
This course is designed to produce tangible artifacts that can be used immediately in graduate school applications, job searches and applications, and professional networking.

1.1 Prerequisites
There are no formal prerequisites, but the course is typically taken in the final year of the B.S. program in Physics, Astrophysics, or Applied Physics.


1.2 Learning Outcomes
You will learn how to:

Write a résumé or CV.
Identify career opportunities, practice networking, and apply to industry jobs.
Choose and apply to graduate M.S. or Ph.D. programs.
Write a research proposal in the style of the NSF GRFP.

Georgia Tech résumé resources
Learn LaTeX in 30 minutes

title: Syllabus
text:
5 Assignments and Grading
There are no exams in this course. Your grade is based primarily on attendance and participation, along with a small number of professional-development assignments. Written assignments should be submitted on Canvas in PDF format by 11:59 PM ET unless otherwise specified.



Assignment
Due Date
Weight




Attendance / participation
Weekly
52%


Résumé
September 11
10%


Online professional presence
October 2
4%


Research proposal, GRFP style
October 23
15%


Personal statement or cover letter
November 20
15%


External participation
December 4
4%




5.1 Assignment Descriptions

Attendance / participation: Attendance is mandatory. Participation will be scored through sign-in sheets and hand-submitted in-class activities. One missed class is dropped from the attendance grade.
Résumé: Prepare or revise a professional résumé suitable for job, internship, research, or graduate school applications.
Online professional presence: Set up a complete LinkedIn profile if you did not have one yet, improve a pre-existing LinkedIn profile, or document appropriate professional use of LinkedIn. GitHub pages, personal websites, or other professional portfolios are also encouraged.
Research proposal: Write a two-page research proposal with references in the style of the NSF Graduate Research Fellowship Program. This assignment should be completed in LaTeX.
Personal statement or cover letter: Write a graduate school personal statement or a job application cover letter, depending on your professional goals.
External participation: Participate in a professional-development event such as a Georgia Tech Career Center workshop, official career fair, industry interview, internship activity, research symposium, conference, grant submission, grant acceptance, or another instructor-approved activity during the semester. Written or photographic documentation of participation is required for credit. Keep an eye on the Georgia Tech Campus Calendar for career-building events. See also: Interviewing resources, Co-ops and internships, Georgia Tech Career Fair.



5.2 Grading Scale
Your final grade will be assigned as a letter grade according to the following scale:



Letter Grade
Percentage




A
90–100%


B
80–89%


C
70–79%


D
60–69%


F
0–59%



Final numeric grades are rounded to the nearest whole percent. For example, 83.76% becomes 84%. This course is not otherwise curved. At Georgia Tech, grades are awarded on a scale of A–F with no plus/minus grades permitted.
Georgia Tech grading system

title: Syllabus
text:
6 Course and Institution Policies

6.1 Attendance
Attendance is mandatory; however, one missed class will be dropped from your grade. You must attend 13 of the 14 scheduled class sessions to receive a full attendance score of 13 × 4% = 52%.
If you will miss a class for any reason, please let me know as soon as possible via email.
To request an excused absence, contact the Dean of Students office for class absence verification in the case of a documented illness, hospitalization, accident, death in the family, family emergency, or lengthy illness.
Dean of Students: Class Attendance

6.1.1 Late Work
Late work is not accepted. An assignment may be excused only through the Dean of Students office. While this is not meant to be a difficult course, all assignments are open and available for the entirety of the semester, therefore appropriate time-management is expected.



6.2 Collaboration and Group Work
Collaboration on assignments is reasonable, as is seeking peer review, feedback, or advice. However, the final product turned in should be the student’s own work. Turned-in assignments that clearly do not reflect the student’s own work will receive zero credit and may be considered a violation of academic integrity.


6.3 Limited Generative AI Use Permitted
Use of generative AI, such as Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and similar tools, is permitted in this course, but only within instructor-approved boundaries. Examples may include drafting assignments, stages of writing, revising work, providing feedback, grammar refinement, or coding assistance.
Georgia Tech OIT guidance on AI

6.3.1 Two Golden Rules

AI usage is entirely optional. You should never feel pressured to use it.
AI should never be used to generate a whole assignment from scratch.

AI usage must be transparent and documented in a required AI Usage Statement with each submission where it is used. The statement should include the following:

The tool used and date of access.
The input prompt used.
A copy of the output.
A brief description of how you used or edited the output.

Because generative AI usage is often iterative, it would be cumbersome to include all prompts and outputs. Therefore, select no more than three of the most consequential or important conversational snippets to include in your statement.
Failure to follow these guidelines, including using generative AI when it is not permitted or failing to disclose its use, may be considered a violation of Georgia Tech’s academic integrity policies. When in doubt, always consult your instructor before using generative AI.



6.4 Academic Integrity
Georgia Tech aims to cultivate a community based on trust, academic integrity, and honor. Students are expected to act according to the highest ethical standards.
Any student suspected of cheating or plagiarizing on a quiz, exam, or assignment will be reported to the Office of Student Integrity, who will investigate the incident and identify the appropriate penalty for violations.
Georgia Tech Academic Honor Code


6.5 Accommodations for Individuals with Disabilities
If you are a student with learning needs that require special accommodation, contact the Office of Disability Services, often referred to as ADAPTS, at (404) 894-2563 or through the Office of Disability Services website as soon as possible to make an appointment to discuss your special needs and to obtain an accommodations letter.
Georgia Tech Office of Disability Services


6.6 Student-Faculty Expectations
At Georgia Tech, we believe that it is important to strive for an atmosphere of mutual respect, acknowledgment, and responsibility between faculty members and the student body.
In the end, simple respect for knowledge, hard work, and cordial interactions will help build the environment we seek. Therefore, I encourage you to remain committed to the ideals of Georgia Tech while in this class.
Georgia Tech student-faculty expectations

title: Assignment 1 · Résumé
text:
Résumés have a long history going back to at least Leonardo da Vinci, who wrote a letter to Ludovico il Moro, Duke of Milan, summarizing his many talents c. 1483–1485.
For this assignment, prepare or revise a professional résumé suitable for use in a job, internship, research, or graduate-school application context. This assignment is intended to produce a polished professional document that you can continue refining and using beyond this course.
Your résumé should present your education, experiences, skills, and accomplishments clearly and strategically for the kinds of opportunities you may pursue after graduation.






Note



For the differences between a résumé and an academic CV, see the Résumé vs. Academic CV supplemental guide.

title: Assignment 1 · Résumé

title: Assignment 1 · Résumé
text:
2 What to Submit

A completed or revised résumé.
The file must be submitted as a PDF.
Upload the PDF directly to this Canvas assignment by the deadline.
If you used generative AI while developing or revising your résumé, include the required AI Usage Statement.

title: Assignment 1 · Résumé
text:
3 Expectations
Your résumé should be professional, readable, and carefully edited. While the precise content will vary, strong résumés typically:

Use clear organization and consistent formatting.
Highlight relevant education, work, research, leadership, service, projects, or technical experience.
Describe experiences in a concise, accomplishment-oriented way.
Use language appropriate for professional or academic opportunities.
Contain no obvious spelling, grammar, or formatting errors.

title: Assignment 1 · Résumé
text:

Georgia Tech Career Center résumé guidance

title: Assignment 1 · Résumé
text:
5 Submission Checklist

I have reviewed the document for clarity, formatting, spelling, and grammar.
I have submitted my résumé as a PDF.
I have included an AI Usage Statement, if needed.

title: Assignment 4 · Personal Statement or Cover Letter
text:
For this assignment, you will prepare a polished application document aligned with one of two possible professional pathways:

A personal statement for graduate-school or research-oriented applications, or
A job cover letter for an industry, government, nonprofit, laboratory, or other professional position.

A document of either type satisfies this assignment. Choose the format most relevant to your current goals.






Important



This assignment must be shaped toward a real opportunity, preferably one you are actually interested in applying for. Generic statements or generic cover letters will not earn a high grade.

title: Assignment 4 · Personal Statement or Cover Letter
text:
For this assignment, you will prepare a polished application document aligned with one of two possible professional pathways:

A personal statement for graduate-school or research-oriented applications, or
A job cover letter for an industry, government, nonprofit, laboratory, or other professional position.

A document of either type satisfies this assignment. Choose the format most relevant to your current goals.






Important



This assignment must be shaped toward a real opportunity, preferably one you are actually interested in applying for. Generic statements or generic cover letters will not earn a high grade.

title: Assignment 4 · Personal Statement or Cover Letter
text:
2 Personal Statement vs. Cover Letter








Document
Primary Purpose
Typical Audience




Personal Statement
Explains your preparation, interests, motivations, and fit for a graduate program, research group, professor, or advanced academic opportunity.
Graduate admissions committees, faculty mentors, or research groups.


Cover Letter
Explains why your skills, background, and interests make you a strong candidate for a specific job posting.
Hiring managers, recruiters, technical team leads, laboratories, companies, or other employers.



Both documents are persuasive professional writing, but they are shaped differently. A personal statement typically emphasizes your academic development, research interests, intellectual goals, and fit with a program or group. A cover letter typically emphasizes your qualifications for a particular role, your relevant experiences, and your ability to contribute to an employer’s needs.

title: Assignment 4 · Personal Statement or Cover Letter
text:
3 Choose One Assignment Track

3.1 Option A: Personal Statement
Write a personal statement directed toward a real graduate-school or research opportunity. Your statement must be shaped toward at least one of the following:

A specific graduate institution or department;
A specific professor, laboratory, or research group;
A specific Ph.D. or M.S. research advertisement or posting.

Your statement should make clear why the opportunity is a good fit for your goals. It should not read as a generic statement that could be sent unchanged to many unrelated programs. A strong personal statement will generally:

Explain your academic or research interests.
Describe relevant preparation, experiences, and skills.
Connect your background to the specific program, faculty member, laboratory, or research opportunity.
Show that you have investigated the program thoroughly rather than writing generically.
Communicate your next-step goals clearly and professionally.



3.2 Option B: Job Cover Letter
Write a cover letter directed toward a real job posting. This may be a posting for employment, an internship, a research technician role, a national laboratory role, an engineering or data position, or another relevant employer. Your letter should be shaped toward the specific job advertisement. A strong cover letter will generally:

Identify the specific position or opportunity.
Explain why you are interested in that role and organization.
Highlight experiences and skills directly relevant to the posting.
Demonstrate that you understand what the employer is seeking.
Communicate professionally and concisely.

title: Assignment 4 · Personal Statement or Cover Letter
Assignment 4 · Personal Statement or Cover Letter
text:
5 What to Submit
Upload to Canvas the following:

Your completed PDF personal statement or job cover letter.
A URL link and/or copy of the relevant advertisement, webpage, department page, or laboratory group page.
An AI Usage Statement, if generative AI was used during your writing or revision process.







Note



The assignment should reflect your own work and professional history. Peer review, instructor feedback, and reasonable collaboration are welcome, but the final submitted document must be your own.

title: Assignment 4 · Personal Statement or Cover Letter
text:
6 Submission Checklist

My document is shaped toward a real career position.
I proofread for clarity, organization, tone, spelling, and grammar.
I submitted the assignment as a single PDF through Canvas.
I included a URL link and/or copy of the relevant job/position information.
I included an AI Usage Statement, if needed.

title: Assignment 5 · External Participation
text:
Primarily, professional development happens outside the classroom. To earn these points, you will participate in and document an external activity during the semester that supports your career preparation, graduate-school preparation, research development, or professional networking.
The purpose of this assignment is to encourage you to take advantage of opportunities at Georgia Tech beyond PHYS 4604 that can help you build experience and advance your professional goals.

title: Assignment 5 · External Participation
text:
Primarily, professional development happens outside the classroom. To earn these points, you will participate in and document an external activity during the semester that supports your career preparation, graduate-school preparation, research development, or professional networking.
The purpose of this assignment is to encourage you to take advantage of opportunities at Georgia Tech beyond PHYS 4604 that can help you build experience and advance your professional goals.

title: Assignment 5 · External Participation
Assignment 5 · External Participation
text:
3 What to Submit
Submit a PDF to Canvas containing documentation of your participation. Your submission should include:

Your name and the title of the event or activity.
The date on which the activity occurred.
A brief 1–2 paragraph description of the activity and how it relates to your professional development.
Written or photographic documentation showing that you participated.

title: Assignment 5 · External Participation
Assignment 5 · External Participation
text:
5 Examples of Qualifying Submissions







Activity
Possible Documentation




Career Center workshop
Registration or attendance confirmation, screenshot, workshop handout, or event photo.


Career Fair
Event registration, fair badge, photo at the event, or another reasonable record of attendance.


Industry interview
Redacted interview invitation or scheduling confirmation.


Research symposium or conference
Program listing, registration confirmation, event badge, photo, or presentation documentation.


Grant submission or acceptance
Submission receipt, confirmation email, award notification, or other appropriate evidence.

title: Assignment 2 · Online Profile
text:
A professional online presence can help you connect with employers, graduate programs, alumni, researchers, and professional communities. You will create or meaningfully improve an online professional profile that represents your background, interests, and goals.
The primary focus of this assignment is LinkedIn. Students who do not yet have a LinkedIn profile should create a complete profile. Students who already have a LinkedIn profile should revise and strengthen that profile.






TipEncouraged, but not required



You may also include links to supplementary professional materials such as a personal website, GitHub profile, digital portfolio, ORCID profile, or other relevant professional presence.

title: Assignment 2 · Online Profile
text:
A professional online presence can help you connect with employers, graduate programs, alumni, researchers, and professional communities. You will create or meaningfully improve an online professional profile that represents your background, interests, and goals.
The primary focus of this assignment is LinkedIn. Students who do not yet have a LinkedIn profile should create a complete profile. Students who already have a LinkedIn profile should revise and strengthen that profile.






TipEncouraged, but not required



You may also include links to supplementary professional materials such as a personal website, GitHub profile, digital portfolio, ORCID profile, or other relevant professional presence.

title: Assignment 2 · Online Profile
text:
2 What to Submit
Submit a single PDF to Canvas that documents your online professional presence. Your submission should include:

A PDF copy of your LinkedIn profile, saved using LinkedIn’s profile export feature.
Optional supplementary URLs to professional websites, portfolios, GitHub, or similar materials that you would like to share. These links are encouraged but are not worth extra points.

title: Assignment 2 · Online Profile
section: 3 How to Save Your LinkedIn Profile as a PDF
text:
3 How to Save Your LinkedIn Profile as a PDF
LinkedIn provides a built-in option to save your profile as a PDF from a desktop browser. To do so:

Log in to LinkedIn on a desktop web browser.
Click the Me icon at the top of the LinkedIn homepage.
Select View Profile.
In the introduction section of your profile, click More or Resources.
Select Save to PDF.

LinkedIn notes that this export feature is available only for profiles set to English. If you are unable to access the PDF export feature, contact your instructor for an appropriate alternative method of documentation.
LinkedIn Help: Save a profile as a PDF

title: Assignment 2 · Online Profile
text:
4 Expectations
Your LinkedIn profile should present you in a clear and professional way. A strong submission will generally include:

A full name and headline.
An introductory summary of yourself.
A location and/or academic or professional affiliation, where appropriate.
An education section that includes Georgia Tech and your degree program.
Relevant work, research, leadership, project, or service experience.
Skills, interests, or areas of professional focus relevant to your goals.

You are encouraged to think about how the profile communicates your likely next steps: industry employment, internships, graduate school, research, or another professional pathway.

title: Assignment 2 · Online Profile
text:
5 Optional Supplementary Professional Links
In addition to your LinkedIn profile, you may include links to other professional materials. These are encouraged, especially when relevant to your field or goals, but they are not required and are not worth extra points.
Examples include:

A personal professional website.
A GitHub profile or other code portfolio.
A design, writing, or project portfolio.
An ORCID profile or other research-facing professional profile.
Another professional platform directly relevant to your career direction.

title: Assignment 2 · Online Profile
text:
6 Submission Checklist

I have checked my profile for clarity, professionalism, spelling, and grammar.
I have saved my LinkedIn profile as a PDF.
I have uploaded the final PDF to Canvas.
I have included any optional URLs I would like to share.

title: Assignment 3 · Research Proposal
text:
For this assignment, you will write a two-page research proposal with references modeled after the National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship Program Graduate Research Plan Statement.
The proposal should be developed with the intention of being ready (or nearly ready) for an actual NSF GRFP submission, should you choose to pursue the fellowship. This means that the proposal should describe a serious, feasible, and clearly motivated graduate-level research direction rather than a vague topic area or informal class exercise.






Important



Undergraduate seniors and B.S. degree holders with no prior graduate enrollment can apply for the NSF GRFP multiple times. However, once enrolled in a graduate program (including joint B.S.-M.S. degree programs), you can only apply once in the first year of your first graduate program.

title: Assignment 3 · Research Proposal
text:
For this assignment, you will write a two-page research proposal with references modeled after the National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship Program Graduate Research Plan Statement.
The proposal should be developed with the intention of being ready (or nearly ready) for an actual NSF GRFP submission, should you choose to pursue the fellowship. This means that the proposal should describe a serious, feasible, and clearly motivated graduate-level research direction rather than a vague topic area or informal class exercise.






Important



Undergraduate seniors and B.S. degree holders with no prior graduate enrollment can apply for the NSF GRFP multiple times. However, once enrolled in a graduate program (including joint B.S.-M.S. degree programs), you can only apply once in the first year of your first graduate program.

title: Assignment 3 · Research Proposal
Assignment 3 · Research Proposal
text:
3 What to Submit
Upload to Canvas the following:

Your completed two-page research proposal with references as a PDF formatted in LaTeX.
An AI Usage Statement, if generative AI was used during the drafting, revision, or formatting of the proposal.

title: Assignment 3 · Research Proposal
text:
4 Proposal Structure
A strong proposal will be specific, carefully reasoned, and shaped toward a plausible graduate research direction. Your proposal may be organized in different ways depending on the project. The following is a starting template:







Section
Purpose




Title
A concise, informative title for the proposed research.


Motivation / Background
Introduce the research problem, its context, and why it matters.


Research Question or Objective
State clearly what the proposed work seeks to understand, test, develop, or accomplish.


Approach / Methods
Describe the proposed strategy, tools, models, experiments, calculations, or analyses.


Intellectual Merit
Explain how the project could advance knowledge, contribute to the field, or address an important scientific or technical challenge.


Broader Impacts
Explain how the proposed work may benefit society or contribute to broader educational, scientific, technological, or community outcomes.


References
Cite relevant literature clearly and consistently.

title: Assignment 3 · Research Proposal
Assignment 3 · Research Proposal
Assignment 3 · Research Proposal
Assignment 3 · Research Proposal
text:
8 Helpful Resources

NSF Graduate Research Fellowship Program
NSF GRFP Statement Requirements
NSF GRFP Funding Opportunity Page

title: Assignment 3 · Research Proposal
text:
9 Submission Checklist

I wrote a two-page research proposal with references.
I included explicit attention to Intellectual Merit.
I included explicit attention to Broader Impacts.
I edited the proposal for clarity, professionalism, and near-submission quality.
I included an AI Usage Statement, if needed.

title: How to Format an AI Usage Statement
text:
Limited use of generative AI tools, such as Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and similar systems, is permitted in PHYS 4604 only within instructor-approved boundaries. Examples of potentially appropriate use may include brainstorming, revising wording, receiving feedback, grammar refinement, or other support tasks connected to the writing process.
When generative AI is used in preparing an assignment submission, students must include an AI Usage Statement with that assignment. The statement documents what tool was used, what was asked of it, what it produced, and how the student incorporated or revised that output.






Note



AI use is entirely optional. You are never required use generative AI in this course.


Overuse of such tools is discouraged as there is growing evidence that reliance on AI can decrease one’s expertise and skills:

“The ADR of standard colonoscopy decreased significantly from 28.4% (226 of 795) before to 22.4% (145 of 648) after exposure to AI, corresponding with an absolute difference of -6.0% (95% CI -10.5 to -1.6; p=0.0089). […] Continuous exposure to AI might reduce the ADR of standard non-AI assisted colonoscopy, suggesting a negative effect on endoscopist behaviour.” (Budzyń et al. 2025)







Important



AI should never be used to generate a whole assignment from scratch. The final submission must reflect your own thinking, judgment, and revisions.

title: How to Format an AI Usage Statement
text:
Limited use of generative AI tools, such as Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and similar systems, is permitted in PHYS 4604 only within instructor-approved boundaries. Examples of potentially appropriate use may include brainstorming, revising wording, receiving feedback, grammar refinement, or other support tasks connected to the writing process.
When generative AI is used in preparing an assignment submission, students must include an AI Usage Statement with that assignment. The statement documents what tool was used, what was asked of it, what it produced, and how the student incorporated or revised that output.






Note



AI use is entirely optional. You are never required use generative AI in this course.


Overuse of such tools is discouraged as there is growing evidence that reliance on AI can decrease one’s expertise and skills:

“The ADR of standard colonoscopy decreased significantly from 28.4% (226 of 795) before to 22.4% (145 of 648) after exposure to AI, corresponding with an absolute difference of -6.0% (95% CI -10.5 to -1.6; p=0.0089). […] Continuous exposure to AI might reduce the ADR of standard non-AI assisted colonoscopy, suggesting a negative effect on endoscopist behaviour.” (Budzyń et al. 2025)







Important



AI should never be used to generate a whole assignment from scratch. The final submission must reflect your own thinking, judgment, and revisions.

title: How to Format an AI Usage Statement
section: 2 When Is an AI Usage Statement Required?
text:
2 When Is an AI Usage Statement Required?
Include an AI Usage Statement with any course submission in which you used generative AI in a meaningful way. This includes, but is not limited to:

Generating ideas, outlines, or possible approaches.
Receiving feedback on a draft.
Revising wording, organization, or clarity.
Using AI for grammar refinement or editing suggestions.
Using AI for coding, LaTeX, or formatting assistance connected to the assignment.

If you did not use generative AI for an assignment, no AI Usage Statement is required.

title: How to Format an AI Usage Statement
section: 3 Required Components of the AI Usage Statement
text:
3 Required Components of the AI Usage Statement
Each AI Usage Statement should include the following four elements:







Required Item
What to Include




1. Tool used and date of access
Name the AI tool and indicate when you used it.


2. Input prompt
Provide the prompt or instruction you entered into the tool.


3. Output received
Include a copy of the AI-generated output relevant to your assignment.


4. How you used or edited it
Briefly explain whether you adopted, rejected, revised, summarized, or otherwise used the output.

title: How to Format an AI Usage Statement
section: 4 What If I Used AI Iteratively?
text:
4 What If I Used AI Iteratively?
Generative AI use is often conversational and iterative. You are not expected to include every single prompt and every output from a long exchange. Instead, include no more than three of the most consequential or important conversational snippets. Select the examples that best show how AI affected your work.
For example, you might include:

A prompt used to generate initial brainstorming ideas.
A prompt asking for feedback on a draft paragraph.
A prompt used to improve clarity or organization in a final revision.

title: How to Format an AI Usage Statement
text:
5 Suggested Format
You may upload the AI Usage Statement as a separate document which accompanies your main submission.

5.1 AI Usage Statement Template
Name, course, and assignment.
AI Tool: [Tool name]
Date of Access: [Date]
Prompt 1:

[Paste the prompt you entered.]

Output 1:

[Paste the relevant AI-generated output.]

How I Used or Edited This Output:

[Briefly explain how the output informed your work, what you changed, and what you did not use.]

title: How to Format an AI Usage Statement
text:
6 What Is Not Appropriate?
The following are not consistent with course policy:

Submitting an assignment that was generated substantially or entirely by AI.
Using AI to invent experiences, qualifications, accomplishments, or participation records.
Using AI in a way that is prohibited by assignment-specific instructions.
Using AI but failing to disclose that use when a statement is required.







TipWhen in doubt



Ask the instructor before using generative AI for a course assignment.

title: How to Format an AI Usage Statement
text:
7 Why This Matters
The AI Usage Statement is not intended to discourage legitimate use of useful tools. Its purpose is to make the writing process transparent and to ensure that submitted work remains your own.
Failure to follow the course AI policy, including using generative AI when it is not permitted or failing to disclose its use, may be considered a violation of Georgia Tech’s academic integrity policies.

title: How to Format an AI Usage Statement
text:
8 References


Budzyń, K. et al. 2025. “Endoscopist Deskilling Risk After Exposure to Artificial Intelligence in Colonoscopy: A Multicentre, Observational Study.” Lancet Gastroenterology & Hepatology 10 (10): 896–903. https://doi.org/10.1016/S2468-1253(25)00133-5.

title: Welcome Page
text:
Fall 2026 | CRN #94323 | 1 credit hour
Fridays, 4:00–4:50 PM • Howey Physics L3 • Weekly in-person attendance required

title: Welcome Page
text:
1 Welcome
Welcome to PHYS 4604: Professional Development. This course is designed to help you prepare for life after your undergraduate degree, whether you are planning to apply to graduate school, pursue industry positions, develop a professional network, or explore multiple possible career paths.
By the end of the semester, you should have several tangible professional materials that you can use immediately, including a résumé or CV, an online professional profile, a research proposal in the style of the NSF Graduate Research Fellowship Program, and either a graduate school personal statement or job cover letter.






Note



Review the full syllabus on Canvas as the home page is a truncated version (with the exception of the Course Schedule which will remain updated here if anything changes during the semester).

title: Welcome Page
text:
2 Course Description
In this course, we will discuss practical skills for post-degree success. Topics include selecting graduate schools, understanding career options with a B.S. or Ph.D., applying to graduate school, applying to industry jobs, developing a professional network, and preparing a research proposal suitable for the NSF Graduate Research Fellowship Program.
There are no formal prerequisites, but the course is typically taken in the final year of the B.S. program in Physics, Astrophysics, or Applied Physics.

2.1 Learning Outcomes

Write a résumé or CV.
Identify career opportunities, practice networking, and apply to industry jobs.
Choose and apply to graduate M.S. or Ph.D. programs.
Write a research proposal in the style of the NSF GRFP.

title: Welcome Page
text:
5 Assignments and Grading
There are no exams in this course. Your grade is based primarily on attendance and participation, along with a small number of professional-development assignments. Written assignments should be submitted on Canvas in PDF format by 11:59 PM ET unless otherwise specified.



Assignment
Due Date
Weight




Attendance / participation
Weekly
52%


Résumé
September 11
10%


Online professional presence
October 2
4%


Research proposal, GRFP style
October 23
15%


Personal statement or cover letter
November 20
15%


External participation
December 4
4%




5.1 Assignment Descriptions

Attendance / participation: Attendance is mandatory. Participation will be scored through sign-in sheets and hand-submitted in-class activities. One missed class is dropped from the attendance grade.
Résumé: Prepare or revise a professional résumé suitable for job, internship, research, or graduate school applications.
Online professional presence: Set up a complete LinkedIn profile, improve a pre-existing LinkedIn profile, or document appropriate professional use of LinkedIn. GitHub pages, personal websites, or other professional portfolios are also encouraged.
Research proposal: Write a two-page research proposal with references in the style of the NSF Graduate Research Fellowship Program. This assignment should be completed in LaTeX.
Personal statement or cover letter: Write a graduate school personal statement or a job application cover letter, depending on your professional goals.
External participation: Participate in a professional-development event such as a Georgia Tech Career Center workshop, official career fair, industry interview, internship activity, research symposium, conference, grant submission, or another instructor-approved activity. Written or photographic documentation is required for credit.

title: Welcome Page
text:
7 A Few Important Course Notes

Attendance is mandatory. One missed class is dropped from the attendance grade. If you will miss class, please email the instructor as soon as possible.
No textbook is required. Course materials and assignment information will be provided through Canvas.
Collaboration is allowed within reason. Peer review, feedback, and advice are appropriate, but submitted work must be your own.
Limited generative AI use is permitted. AI may be used only within instructor-approved boundaries and must be documented with an AI Usage Statement when used.
When in doubt, ask. If you are unsure whether a resource, collaboration, or AI use is appropriate, contact the instructor before submitting.







TipNext steps



Review the full syllabus on Canvas, then take the Syllabus Quiz. Note the major assignment due dates listed in the Course Schedule and begin thinking about which post-degree pathway you want to prepare for this semester.

8.1 AI Disclosure
Some course materials were developed with assistance from generative AI tools, including Claude (Anthropic), Copilot (Microsoft), ChatGPT (OpenAI), and Gemini (Google). All AI-generated content was reviewed, edited, and approved by the instructor before use.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Course Policies — The Short Version

Attendance is mandatory. One missed class is dropped from your attendance grade.
No textbook. Materials are provided through Canvas/course website.
No exams.
Limited AI use is permitted but must be disclosed with an AI Usage Statement.
Late work is not accepted. All assignments are open the entire semester, so plan ahead.
When in doubt, ask. Email or office hours, I’d rather you ask than guess.

title: Course Overview & Résumés

title: Course Overview & Résumés
section: The Career Fair Is Seventeen Days Away
text:
The Career Fair Is Seventeen Days Away
The Fall 2026 All Majors Career Fair is September 14–15 at Georgia Tech.

Georgia Tech Career Fair
Georgia Tech Interviewing Resources

Your résumé is due September 11 before the fair begins.

The goal of today’s lecture: covering what goes on a résumé and how to start writing one if you haven’t done so already.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Part III: Résumés
A résumé is typically organized into six sections:

Contact information
Education
Experience
Skills
Projects and leadership
Awards and honors (optional)

Not every résumé looks the same. The order and emphasis depend on your background and the opportunity.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Résumé vs. Academic CV









Résumé
Academic CV




Length
1–2 pages
No limit; grows over time


Purpose
Fit for a specific role
Full academic and professional record


Used for
Jobs, internships, most undergrad opportunities
Grad fellowships, faculty positions, postdocs


Customization
Tailored to each application
Updated over time



For most of you a résumé is your concern. A CV will come later during graduate school, as publications, talks, and grants accumulate.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Contact Information
Make this easy to find — usually at the top of the page.

Full name (prominent — consider a slightly larger font)
Professional email address (GT email is fine)
Phone number
LinkedIn profile URL
GitHub or portfolio URL (if you have one worth sharing)
City and state — no full street address needed


Mention that GT email is appropriate for now but a permanent personal email is useful long-term. LinkedIn URL should be customized (linkedin.com/in/yourname) rather than the default random string.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Education

Georgia Institute of Technology

B.S. Physics / Astrophysics / Applied Physics (or joint degree)
Expected graduation: May 2027 (or your actual date)
GPA: include if ≥ 3.0 — judgement call

Relevant coursework (optional, useful if experience is thin)
Academic honors: Dean’s List, scholarships, etc.

List your most recent degree first. If you attended another institution, include it.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Experience
The most important section for most employers.
Include: work, research, internships, co-ops, teaching assistantships
For each entry:

Organization name and your role or title
Dates — month and year (e.g., May 2025 – Aug. 2025)
Location (city, state) — or “Remote”
2–4 bullet points describing what you did and what it produced

Research experience counts strongly for physics students: faculty advisors, national labs, REUs.

Emphasize that even a one-semester research role is worth including if it involved real technical work.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Writing Strong Bullets
The most common mistake: describing duties instead of accomplishments.







Weak
Strong




Helped with research in the lab
Analyzed detector calibration data in Python; results incorporated into weekly group reports


Responsible for data analysis
Developed signal-processing pipeline in MATLAB, reducing analysis time by 40%


Worked on simulations
Implemented Monte Carlo simulation of particle trajectories in C++; validated against published results



Lead with a strong action verb. Quantify where you can.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Strong Action Verbs
Use these to open your bullet points:
Research and Analysis Analyzed · Modeled · Simulated · Measured · Characterized · Derived · Validated
Development and Implementation Built · Designed · Developed · Implemented · Programmed · Automated · Optimized
Communication and Collaboration Presented · Wrote · Documented · Led · Collaborated · Mentored · Coordinated

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Skills
List technical skills — the specific tools and languages you actually know.
Programming and Software Python, MATLAB, C/C++, Julia, Mathematica, LaTeX, Git, Linux/bash, etc.
Laboratory and Instrumentation List specific instruments, techniques, or equipment relevant to your work
Other Data analysis, scientific writing, numerical methods

Do not list generic software (Word, PowerPoint) unless the role specifically requires it.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Projects and Leadership
Valuable if work or research experience is limited and generally worth including.
Technical projects

Computational or experimental class projects with real results
Independent projects, open-source contributions

Leadership and service

Club officers, SPS (Society of Physics Students), outreach programs
Teaching or peer tutoring roles

Describe these the same way you describe experience: action verb, specific contribution, result if possible.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Formatting
Keep it clean and professional.

One page for most undergraduates (two pages with substantial experience)
Consistent fonts — one or two at most; no smaller than 10 pt for body text
Adequate white space — do not cram everything in
Consistent alignment of dates and section headers
No photos, no graphics, no colored text (with rare exceptions)
Typos and grammar errors are disqualifying. Proofread. Then proofread again.

Georgia Tech Career Center — Résumé Guidance

Encourage students to use one if they are starting from scratch. The formatting rules exist because recruiters scan résumés in seconds.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
Upcoming Schedule

Start your résumé draft — or revisit the one you have.
Bring it to class next Friday (September 4) — we will workshop drafts in class.
Résumé is due: September 11 at 11:59 PM ET — submitted as PDF to Canvas.
Career Fair: September 14–15 — Attendance counts as possible External Participation credit.

title: Course Overview & Résumés
text:
References

title: Résumé vs. Academic CV
text:
A résumé is a concise, targeted document that highlights the experiences and qualifications most relevant to a particular opportunity. It is the standard document for most internships, industry jobs, research technician positions, and many early-career applications.
An academic curriculum vitae, usually called a CV, is a longer and more comprehensive record of academic, research, teaching, and professional accomplishments. It grows over time and is commonly used for graduate school, academic research positions, postdoctoral applications, faculty positions, grant applications, and promotion or review processes.






Important



Most undergraduates will need a strong résumé well before they need a true academic CV. An academic CV is often first developed during graduate school, when publications, talks, research outputs, teaching roles, and professional service begin to accumulate.

title: Résumé vs. Academic CV
text:
A résumé is a concise, targeted document that highlights the experiences and qualifications most relevant to a particular opportunity. It is the standard document for most internships, industry jobs, research technician positions, and many early-career applications.
An academic curriculum vitae, usually called a CV, is a longer and more comprehensive record of academic, research, teaching, and professional accomplishments. It grows over time and is commonly used for graduate school, academic research positions, postdoctoral applications, faculty positions, grant applications, and promotion or review processes.






Important



Most undergraduates will need a strong résumé well before they need a true academic CV. An academic CV is often first developed during graduate school, when publications, talks, research outputs, teaching roles, and professional service begin to accumulate.

title: Résumé vs. Academic CV
text:
2 When Should You Use Each?








Situation
Usually Use
Why




Internship or industry job application
Résumé
Employers typically want a concise, tailored summary of relevant skills and experience.


Career fair or networking conversation
Résumé
A short document is easier to scan quickly and use in conversation.


Undergraduate research position
Usually résumé
A résumé is typically sufficient unless the program specifically asks for a CV.


Graduate school application
Résumé or CV
Follow the program instructions. Research-intensive programs may request a CV.


Graduate fellowship, academic research role, or postdoctoral application
Academic CV
These applications often need a fuller record of research, publications, presentations, and honors.


Faculty jobs, academic promotion, grant applications
Academic CV
The CV serves as a comprehensive professional record.

title: Résumé vs. Academic CV
text:
3 Key Differences








Feature
Résumé
Academic CV




Typical length
Often 1 page for undergraduates; sometimes 2 pages with substantial experience
No fixed limit; grows with academic experience


Purpose
Market your fit for a specific role
Document your full academic and professional record


Customization
Highly tailored to the job, internship, or opportunity
Usually updated over time, but less radically tailored


Style
Selective, concise, skill-oriented
Comprehensive, chronological, detailed


Common content
Education, skills, experience, projects, leadership, selected awards
Education, appointments, publications, talks, teaching, grants, awards, mentoring, service, outreach, professional memberships


Main question answered
“Why are you a strong candidate for this position?”
“What is the full scope of your academic and professional record?”

title: Résumé vs. Academic CV
text:
4 What Belongs on a Résumé?
A résumé should be selective. You do not need to list everything you have ever done. Instead, choose the experiences most relevant to the opportunity you are pursuing.

4.1 Common Résumé Sections

Contact information
Education
Relevant work, research, or internship experience
Projects or technical experience
Skills, especially software, laboratory, computational, or language skills
Leadership, service, or activities, when relevant
Selected honors or awards, if useful







NotePHYS 4604 résumé assignment



Most, if not all, students should submit a résumé rather than an academic CV. The goal is to prepare a polished professional document that can be used for jobs, internships, research opportunities, career fairs, or related applications.

title: Résumé vs. Academic CV
section: 5 What Belongs on an Academic CV?
text:
5 What Belongs on an Academic CV?
An academic CV is much broader than a résumé. The three example CVs provided for this course show that academic CVs can vary in style and length, but they commonly document a wide range of academic contributions.

5.1 Common Academic CV Sections

Contact information and professional title
Education, often including dissertation or advisor information
Academic employment or appointments
Research interests or areas of specialization
Publications, sometimes separated into peer-reviewed articles, book chapters, reports, and works in progress
Conference presentations, invited talks, and seminars
Grants, fellowships, awards, and honors
Teaching experience, often with courses, terms, enrollment, or instructional roles
Mentoring and student supervision
Professional service, such as reviewing, organizing conferences, committee work, and society involvement
Institutional service and public outreach

title: Résumé vs. Academic CV
text:
6 Practical Guidance for Students

Start with a résumé. Every student should be able to produce a polished résumé for jobs, internships, research roles, and networking.
Begin a CV only when it becomes useful. This often happens during graduate school, or earlier if you begin applying for research-intensive programs that specifically request one.
Follow the instructions of the opportunity. If an application asks for a résumé, submit a résumé. If it asks for a CV, submit a CV.
Do not simply rename one document. A résumé and CV serve different purposes and should be structured accordingly.
Maintain a master record. Even if you are submitting a one-page résumé, keep a private running list of all research projects, presentations, awards, teaching roles, and service. That record can later become the basis of an academic CV.







Tip



For most students in PHYS 4604, the immediate priority is learning to produce a strong résumé. Understanding the academic CV now will help you recognize when and how to build one later.
